arabic_adaptation_note: Earliest stage of the Fusha ladder: recognising letters, their joined shapes, and the short vowels. Mapped to the bottom of the zero-to-fluency roadmap.

descriptors.reading: Recognises Arabic letters and a few whole words by shape.
descriptors.grammar: No explicit grammar yet; whole words are learned as units.
descriptors.morphology: No segmentation awareness; a word is a single shape.
descriptors.vocabulary: A small set of very common words.

learner_capabilities: read a few high-frequency whole words
forbidden_overclaims: this level is a teaching scaffold, not an official CEFR certification
it does not assess or certify the learner

arabic_adaptation_note: Beginner: simple words and a few set phrases; spelling variants noticed but not analysed.

descriptors.reading: Reads simple familiar words and short set phrases.
descriptors.writing: Writes familiar words and very short phrases.
descriptors.syntax: Recognises that words combine, without naming roles.

forbidden_overclaims: this level is a teaching scaffold, not an official CEFR certification
it does not assess or certify the learner

arabic_adaptation_note: Elementary: basic nominal and verbal sentences, common particles, simple attached pronouns and prepositions — named gently, without iʿrāb terminology.

descriptors.reading: Reads short simple sentences on familiar topics.
descriptors.writing: Writes simple connected phrases and short sentences.
descriptors.grammar: Recognises a basic nominal vs verbal sentence; common particles named plainly.
descriptors.morphology: Notices a prefixed particle or the article without formal analysis.

learner_capabilities: recognise common particles and the article
forbidden_overclaims: this level is a teaching scaffold, not an official CEFR certification
it does not assess or certify the learner

arabic_adaptation_note: Intermediate: routine Fusha sentences; common case/mood named lightly; basic root/pattern awareness begins.

descriptors.reading: Reads routine Fusha texts on familiar matters.
descriptors.writing: Writes connected text on familiar topics.
descriptors.grammar: Common case/mood are named (not deeply analysed); basic agreement noticed.
descriptors.syntax: Recognises common subordination and the basic sentence types.

forbidden_overclaims: this level is a teaching scaffold, not an official CEFR certification
it does not assess or certify the learner

arabic_adaptation_note: Upper-intermediate: longer sentences, common subordination, richer morphology; explicit grammar correction is appropriate.

descriptors.reading: Reads longer Fusha texts and follows argument.
descriptors.writing: Writes clear detailed text with subordination.
descriptors.grammar: Case/mood explained explicitly; governing particles recognised.
descriptors.morphology: Roots, patterns, and derivative shapes used actively.
descriptors.syntax: Handles common subordinate and relative clauses.

forbidden_overclaims: this level is a teaching scaffold, not an official CEFR certification
it does not assess or certify the learner

arabic_adaptation_note: Advanced: complex Classical/Fusha prose; nuanced particles; governor/dependency reasoning and detailed iʿrāb appear.

descriptors.reading: Reads complex Classical/Fusha prose with nuance.
descriptors.morphology: Fine morphological detail, including derivative types.
descriptors.syntax: Resolves layered subordination and governing particles.
descriptors.vocabulary: Rich, including less common Classical vocabulary.

forbidden_overclaims: this level is a teaching scaffold, not an official CEFR certification
it does not assess or certify the learner

arabic_adaptation_note: Mastery: advanced rhetorical and Classical usage; fine-grained syntactic ambiguity and detailed iʿrāb discussion across all diagnostic classes.

descriptors.reading: Reads virtually any Fusha/Classical text with ease.
descriptors.grammar: Full iʿrāb and fine-grained ambiguity handled.
descriptors.syntax: Resolves fine syntactic ambiguity and rhetorical structure.

forbidden_overclaims: this level is a teaching scaffold, not an official CEFR certification
it does not assess or certify the learner
